era_context: The Late Bronze Age is in acute crisis. The year before the traditional dating of Ramesses III's Sea Peoples victory, this year falls within the period of maximum instability across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. Major empires are fragmenting, trade networks are collapsing, and large-scale population movements are underway. This is the nadir of the Bronze Age collapse, a period of rapid, cascading state failures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe (no written records; archaeological evidence sparse)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited synchronous documentation)
East Asia (contemporary Chinese records exist but are rarely cross-referenced with Mediterranean events)
The Americas (no contact with Old World; no synchronous documentation of this year)

events.title: Continued Sea Peoples incursions and migrations across the Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: The year before Ramesses III's major victory, the Sea Peoples are actively raiding and migrating across the Eastern Mediterranean. Multiple groups (Peleset, Tjeker, Shekelesh, Denyen, Weshesh) are moving by sea and land, attacking coastal settlements and attempting to establish footholds in the Levant and Egypt. These are not coordinated invasions but rather a series of raids and migration attempts by displaced populations and mercenary groups. The Egyptian military is mobilised to defend against these incursions, and the pressure on Egypt's borders is intense.
key_figures: Ramesses III (Pharaoh of Egypt, preparing defences)

sources.name: Medinet Habu temple reliefs and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: The Sea Peoples incursions are well-attested in Egyptian sources, but the specific dating of individual raids to -1186 versus -1185 or -1184 is uncertain. The assignment to -1186 is approximate, reflecting the broader period of incursions.
cross_references: Leads to the Battle of the Delta in -1185

events.title: Destruction and abandonment of major Levantine cities continues
events.description: The destruction of Levantine cities that began in the late 13th century continues. Cities such as Sidon and other coastal settlements are experiencing raids, destruction, and population displacement. The archaeological record shows evidence of burning, abandonment, and the disruption of settlement patterns. The causes are multiple: Sea Peoples raids, the collapse of the Hittite Empire (which had provided some regional stability), and the breakdown of long-distance trade networks. Survivors are fleeing inland or to Egypt, seeking refuge.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Levantine sites (Sidon, Tell Kazel, etc.)

events.certainty_note: The destruction of Levantine cities is archaeologically attested but not precisely dated to individual years. The assignment to -1186 is conventional, reflecting the broader period of collapse (c. -1200 to -1150 BCE).
cross_references: Part of the broader Late Bronze Age collapse

events.title: Hittite Empire in final stages of collapse; internal instability and external pressure
events.description: The Hittite Empire is experiencing its final years of coherence. Internal civil strife, invasion by the Kaska peoples from the north, and pressure from the Sea Peoples are fragmenting the state. The capital Hattusa is either under siege or about to be abandoned. The empire's administrative system is breaking down, and regional governors are losing contact with the centre. Within a few years, the Hittite state will cease to exist as a major power.
key_figures: Suppiluliuma II (last known Great King of the Hittites)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Hattusa and other Hittite sites

events.certainty_note: The fall of the Hittite Empire is archaeologically attested but not precisely dated. Scholarly estimates range from -1200 to -1150 BCE. The assignment to -1186 is approximate.
cross_references: Part of the Late Bronze Age collapse; contemporary with Sea Peoples incursions and the fragmentation of Mycenaean Greece

events.title: Mycenaean Greece in advanced stages of collapse; palace centres destroyed or abandoned
events.description: The major palatial centres of Mycenaean Greece (Pylos, Tiryns, Mycenae) are destroyed or in the process of being abandoned. The destruction is attributed to a combination of internal conflict, invasion (possibly by Dorian Greeks from the north), and the broader destabilisation of the Eastern Mediterranean. The loss of centralised administration, literacy (Linear B), and long-distance trade marks the transition from the Bronze Age to the Early Iron Age. Population displacement and migration are occurring, with some Greeks moving to Cyprus and the Levantine coast.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenaean palace sites

events.certainty_note: The destruction of Mycenaean palaces is archaeologically confirmed but not precisely dated to individual years. Scholarly estimates range from -1200 to -1150 BCE. The assignment to -1186 is approximate.
cross_references: Part of the Late Bronze Age collapse; contemporary with the fall of the Hittite Empire and Sea Peoples incursions

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of events in -1186 is highly uncertain. The archaeological record does not provide year-by-year resolution; instead, it shows destruction layers and abandonment phases that span decades. The assignment of specific events to -1186 versus -1185 or -1187 is largely conventional and reflects scholarly consensus chronologies that themselves have ±10–20 year uncertainties. The 'Sea Peoples' are often portrayed as a unified threat, but modern scholarship views them as a complex phenomenon. The role of climate change (drought, famine) in the collapse is increasingly emphasised, but the relative weight of climate versus military/political factors remains debated. Some scholars argue that the 'collapse' narrative overstates the uniformity and speed of change; Egypt and some Levantine cities experienced disruption but continuity, while others experienced more severe state failure.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -1186 is extremely sparse. No specific events are dated to this year in contemporary sources; instead, scholars assign events to this year based on archaeological stratigraphy and chronological calculations. The Egyptian sources (Medinet Habu, Papyrus Harris I) are the most detailed, but they are propagandistic and do not provide year-by-year narrative. The Hittite and Mycenaean records are fragmentary and do not provide precise dating. The perspective of the Sea Peoples themselves is almost entirely lost; they are known only through Egyptian accounts and archaeological evidence. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, Central Asia, and East Asia are absent from the surviving record. The chronological synchronisation between Egyptian, Hittite, and Aegean chronologies remains uncertain by ±10–20 years, making the assignment of events to specific years highly provisional.

graph_edges.note: The Sea Peoples incursions of -1186 lead to the major invasion attempt and Ramesses III's victory in -1185
The destruction of Levantine cities is contemporary with Sea Peoples incursions
The collapse of the Hittite Empire removes a stabilising power and accelerates the disruption of the Levant
The collapse of Mycenaean Greece is contemporary with Sea Peoples incursions; both are part of the same destabilisation
